But first things first ... below are two solo email ads.
The goal was to drive people to our squeeze page AND also pre-sell them on
wanting the produet In this market (weight loss) you can get away with doing things like
that. If you're in a less rabid market, it maynot always be hest (in which case just go for
capturing the op t in and talking up the bribe, etc -- but there is no "one-size-fits-all" with
this sort of thing). By the way, a bit of context: We ran these ads with a company called
'1\rcamax." This is a compiled emaillist of people who have subscribed to get random
information sent to them (cartoons, whatever) and they agreed to also get 3rd party
solicitations, too. So not exactly the most targeted list, but the sheer number of emails you

Subject line: How to lose belly fat while eating "bad" foods
New research by a world class fitness expert and bestselling
weight loss author reveals a very bizarre 	(but effective) way to
realistically drop 10-20 lbs in 30 days while still eating all the
foods you love.

quickly and safely losing weight while still eating ALL the foods
·you love 	(including burgers, 	chocolate, 	cakes, 	pies, 	french fries

[LINK]
How does it work?
It's based on a secret way of consuming protein that puts your body
into "nutrient storing mode" 	instead of "fat storing mode".
This means, 	even if you eat "bad foods" 	you won't store fat.
Instead, 	the opposite happens:
Your body seeks out and stores the nutrients in those foods,
leaving you lighter, 	thinner and a size smaller very quickly.
Within 30 days of doing this diet you can realistically lose as
much as 20-30 lbs 	(the beavier you are the faster it works).
Hare are some interesting facts about this diet:
* Based on hard science - 	proven to work even for women who can't
lose weight no matter how much they diet or exercise.

So... which onc won?
And why?
Well, the "why" is always subjective. But in terms of sales they were virtually
identical. This surprised me more than anyone. The shorter one (test B) got way more
clicks but, get this, the longer ad (test A) got more LEADS per click (i.e. far more of the
people who elieked over to our squeeze page became opt ins and, since the sales were
virtually idcntical, that mcans thc longer one won hig time-- especially since those leacis
arc now on our list and I'm sending them an email every day). So what does this mean,
exactly? In this case, the longer ad produced more leads. This is not really all that
uncommon, by the way. This is onc rcason why I am not a hig fan of obsessing over
clickthrus and open rates. As I've said before lo these many times: You can get really high
open rates and clickthrus and make less sales/ generate less overallleads. Such as is the
case in this test. Again, the shorter ad won hands down on clickthrus. But it gencrated less
overall opt ins and, over time, will have very likely generated way less sales as a result (we
shall see, but that's what it's looking like so far).
But that ain't all.
Herc's something else that occurred tome after running this test:
A few days before running this test, I wrote an email to our list that got an
exceptionally large amount of sales. Large as in 2-3 times more sales than we normally
would get on any given day. (This is a good reason to write fresh emails -- at least for
several months -- bcfore loading everything up in an auto-responder if you have thc time
and patience. You can gauge things like this instead of wondering why you had a h uge
spike in sales since all your leacis would be on different days of the cycle.) Anyway, where
was I? Oh yes ... about that email. I t did extremely well and since the longer test ad won
(in terms of leacis and sales, but not clickthrus) I thought to myself, "Hey Ben, why don't
you test that email as a solo email ad? Just reformat it a bit and see what happens?" And
so that's just what I'm gonna do. I have not run it yet, but when I do it will probably be to
a smaller, more targeted list than Arcamax (yes, I also realized what I should have done

ezine publishers mayevennot let us do it (we'll see). Another thing to note is the "advice

and, hopefully, trust. In this case, enough trust to click our link, opt in and, eventually buy.

OK, so enough of solo ads for now.
Let's sally forth onto something else I've been doing in email that's reaping hig $$:
Behold The ''Ancient History E111ail''
One kind of email that's fast becoming a money-maker (in multiple markets I use it
in) is what I call the "ancient history" email. This is where you titillate and fascinate your
reader with an interesting history lesson and segue in to selling your product. I like to do
this with the Bible whenever possihle. Why? Because the majority of people I sell to own a
Bible. They may or may not he Christian, but chances are they have a dusty old Bible
somewhere. And since they own one, it gives my email immediate context and relevance
to their lives (even if the relevance is a very small connection,just the faet they own a
Bible or, at the very least, know what it is). I rememher even the great copywriter Clayton
Makepeace talking about using Bihlical terminology in his ads for similar reasons.
Anyway, below is an example of how I did this selling a fricncl's hook on increasing sex
drive. He's a genius at sex drive and relationship stuff, but he's a marketing moron and
clueless when it comes to selling on the Internet (checking his email is about all he knows
how to do). So he asked me for help writing a series of emails andthisis the "ancient
history" one I wrote for him to sell to a ]V partner's list. See if you can't model it for
whatever you sell (and if you need a connection to the Bible for your product, just Google,
"what does the Bihle say about __" and fill in your topic or a relatcd topic):

First off, this was an email sent to my colleague'sJV partner list-- an affiliate email
(1'11 talk more about affiliate marketing here in a minute). And while I am not sure how
many o f the emails I wrote for him were used by his JV partner, I do know they
collectively garneredalmost an 8% conversion rate (clickthrus to sales) to someone else's
list. (Not too shabby!) Also notice how the entire email stays inside the "world" of the man
suffering from lack of sex drive. No doubt many women would find this email offensive,
but I'm not writing to her. l'm writing about HIM, HIS problems, and what HE'S going
through-- told in a simple, fun formatthat should be very hard for him to NOT keep
reading. Again, I like using the Bible for this for the aforementioned reasons. But any
ancient history context can get the job done. And I've been doing this for other markets,
too. So ean you --just use theabove as a "template", fillin your market/produet details,
Google a connection between what you sell and the Bible and wateh what happens ...
Speaking Of Affiliate Marketing ...
Some Email Players have been anxious for me to talk about using email to ratebet
up affiliate marketing profits. There's no way I eould do this justiee in one issue or even
probably 12 issues. So 1'11 revisit it periodically (if you have specific affiliate marketing
questions related to email, just let m e know). In the meantime, here are some tips you ean
take to the bank (hey, pun intended ... ). Mostly, there is NO difference between writing
emails for produets your own or produets you sell as an affiliate. But there are some things
you can do to put the odds more in your favor (give yourself "house odds" so to speak).
Only Sell Produets You Use/Believe In
Really, you should only sell produets you believe in and use (in my humble, but
accurate, opinion). Or, at least be selling produets created by people you can personally

Forget The One Shot Emalls
Here's another hig tip: Don'tjust send out one email and then quit. If you're going
to sell a produet via affiliate marketing then spend at LEAST a good week on it -- every
day. Daily emails, eaeh building on each other, can equallots more of the green stufT in
your hot litde packet. Last summer I mailed for my colleague Jason Leister. I t was for a
paid tele-seminar he did about freelancing mistakes. Now, you may think this violates my
first rule above about only mailing for produets you use and believe in (since the tele-
seminar had not yet ran when I mailed for it). But the thing is, I believe in whatjason
teaches. I read his first eBook about getting freelancing clients and i t was not only
awesome, but it helperl me land clients and negotiate (and made me an overall better
salesman). So l'm kind of ajason Leister "fanboy" when it eomes to freelaneing. That
wcek was a 4 day week so I sent 4 emails out to my list -- all in a row -- promoting his tele-
seminar to my modest-sized list. The results? Here's what he told me in an email:

and not "how much money can I make!" -- the difference in your sales will be night and
day, not only up front but over time as your list realizes you really DO have their hest
interest at hcart). So mail hard, mail often and go for the gold.
Track Results So You Can
hnprove Sales Even MORE Next Tim.e
Depending on how you're set up, your tracking ability maybeabit Jimited mailing
other peoples' offers. I use lShoppingCart and they have a decent enough tracking
mechanism where I can track clicks. What I do is I ask the publisher to tell me how many
sales wc got and tigure out the conversion (clicks to sales). It helps if you're friends with
the publisher. So in the case of Michael Senoff, when I mail his Eugene Schwartz deal (I
mail it once or twice per year) we're able to see if changes and tweaks we make to the
emails (I use more or less the same emails each time, but like to play around with new
ideas as well) are working or not. Again, if you got a great offer, there's nothing wrong
with mailing it again later. You already wrote the emails and did the hard work, after all.
Why not use them again?
N egotiate Better
Deals For YOUR List
Anothcr profit boosting idea that will also endear your list to you more: Negotiate a
BETTER deal from the publisher for your list than others get. You won't always be able
to do this, of course. And again, it helps if you're friends with the publisher. But if you
can pull it off, make sure you tell your list about it in the emails (if you don't tell them,

Subject Line: When never to use benefits in selling
Ben,
•
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 30 of 32 --

.
•
Yes, 	it's true •
Benefits can hurt your chances of landing new clients 	(and this
goes for ANY kind of client --not j ust copywriting clients) . 	I
know this goes against established goo-roo orthodoxy, 	but it's the
truth.
Prove it, 	you say?
OK, 	take my early misadventures for example.
Back when I 	was first trying to get copywriting clients, 	I would
come out "swinging" 	in my ads and on the phone trying to sell my
services.
I'd pour those benefits on THICK.
I'd make guarantees.
And big claims.
And ooze benefits on the phone.
The result?
I'd rarely ever get the gig.
And the reason why is because selling is not pitching .
!t's selling.
All my benefits did was create MORE objections.
Then one day, 	I 	changed my tune and started asking questions,
instead of pitching benefits. 	Instead of saying, 	"My oopywriting
can increase your sales by 50% 1" I 	instead asked things like, 	"how
are you guys doing with emails, 	are you getting good response?" or
"What made you think I was the right person to talk to about this?"
And then just let them talk.
I 	stopped pitching and started selling.
Big difference in response, 	too.
I wish I 	could say I was a genius .
But the reality is, 	I 	learned this from my colleague Jason Leister
who is a master at getting clients and profiting from existing
clients.
He got me straight on a 	lot of this stuff •
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 31 of 32 --
